caption:
6 Next.js cache patterns that will save your production.

Next.js 15 changed everything about caching. No more magic. You control what caches, when it expires, and how to bust it.

If you're still using the old defaults, your app is stale.

📌 SAVE this cache reference.

Which pattern surprised you? 👇

#nextjs #react #webdev #coding #programming #frontend #developer #caching #nextjs15 #codingtips

slides.title: Next.js cache patterns that save your production
slides.subtitle: Next.js 15 changed everything. No more magic caching.

slides.title: fetch is no longer cached by default
slides.desc: In Next.js 15, fetch() defaults to no-cache. You must opt in.
slides.punchline: The biggest breaking change in Next.js 15.

slides.title: revalidateTag — surgical cache busting
slides.desc: Tag your cached data. Invalidate by tag when data changes.
slides.punchline: Invalidate exactly what changed. Nothing else.

slides.title: revalidatePath — bust an entire route
slides.desc: When the whole page needs to refresh. Nuclear option.

slides.title: unstable_cache — cache anything
slides.desc: Not just fetch — cache database queries, computations, anything.
slides.punchline: Cache DB queries with tags and TTL.

slides.title: React cache() — deduplicate per request
slides.desc: Multiple components call the same function? It runs once.

slides.title: ISR — regenerate on demand
slides.desc: Static pages that update without redeploy. The best of both worlds.

items.label: fetch no-cache
items.desc: Default in Next.js 15 — opt in to cache

items.label: revalidateTag
items.desc: Bust cache by tag from Server Actions

slides.teaser: Next: Server Actions — the pattern that killed API routes
